seed_problem: Schedule a DAG of instructions onto p pipelines minimizing cycles under latency/dependency constraints (list-scheduling is a trap); baseline = topological order. compiler domain.
mutation: composite: keep the min-cycles latency-constrained DAG scheduling goal but couple THREE per-bundle resource mechanisms into one feasibility region -- (1) per-kind functional-unit caps cap_t AND a global issue width W, plus (2) a shared register-file read-port budget P where kind t costs rp_t ports. The read-port budget cross-couples the functional-unit kinds (two ops that each fit their own unit cap may still be un-bundleable because their read ports collide), so a good schedule must balance latency-critical packing against port pressure, not just fill unit slots. Objective = makespan; baseline = one-op-per-cycle program-order schedule.
title: VLIW Backend: Latency-Constrained Bundle Scheduling with Read-Port Pressure
strategies: sequential baseline: one operation per cycle in program (topological) order, spacing to satisfy latencies -> exactly the grader's B construction, ratio ~0.1

in-order list scheduling: walk operations in program order, drop each into the earliest cycle whose issue width, unit caps and read-port budget still admit it -> packs bundles but is trapped when a cheap op steals a slot a critical op needed

critical-path priority list scheduling: compute latency-weighted bottom levels, step cycle by cycle and among ready ops pack the most critical first under all three resource limits -> shortens the makespan-gating path and diverges from in-order packing on trap/needle tests
invalid: issue every operation in cycle 0 -> violates latencies and per-bundle resources, infeasible (0)
